condition.selected: If I know 5 × 7, I should still compute 7 × 5 separately to be sure

incorrects.description: Student selected option B: computing both facts separately

params.description: The fact pair 3 × 8 = 24 and 8 × 3 = 24 pulses gently.

steps.text: You're working on it. The key is that you don't need to compute both. Look at 3 × 8 and 8 × 3. They give the same product. One fact tells you both answers.

condition.selected: The strategy only helps when I have an array in front of me

incorrects.description: Student selected option C: limiting strategy to visual contexts

params.description: Text '3 facts learned → 6 facts known' pulses.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The commutative property works with any multiplication, not just arrays. Whether you see a picture or just numbers, 5 × 7 and 7 × 5 both equal 35.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A highlights: 'If I know 5 × 7, I already know 7 × 5 — no extra work'.

steps.text: Here, let me show you. The strategy is about saving work. When you know 5 × 7 equals 35, you automatically know 7 × 5 equals 35 too. No extra computing needed. The answer is the first option. That is what makes it a strategy, it lets you skip work you would otherwise have to do.
